narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 45.8% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic indicators like your undergraduate performance and early law school grades, while setting aside factors like gender or racial background. The most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting failure was your third-year decile ranking of 3, which placed you lower in your class than the average of nearly 6 for students who went on to pass the bar. This stood out as the top concern because stronger performance in later years often signals the sustained academic stamina needed for bar success. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since it aligns reasonably well with passers' averages around 3.24, showing a solid foundation from your prior studies. Similarly, your first-year decile of 5 provided another helpful boost, as it was closer to the typical 6.04 for successful students, indicating you started strong. Other aspects, like your middle family income bracket—which is common among bar passers—and your full-time enrollment plans, which match the norm for 93% of those who succeed, played supportive but less decisive roles. Compared to admitted students, your LSAT score of 29 is notably below their average of 37, which may have indirectly amplified concerns from your later-year grades. While this outcome is disappointing, especially given your early promise and commitment to full-time study, it highlights where the model saw the biggest risk in your trajectory. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score toward the 37+ range typical for passers, and focus on elevating your class rankings in any future opportunities through targeted study habits or tutoring. You might also explore programs with bar passage support or gather letters emphasizing your resilience from your undergraduate days. With these steps, you can reshape your profile and reapply stronger—many students turn predictions like this into success stories.
